title: Gogebic County, Michigan

infobox.value: ([/ɡoʊˈɡiːbɪk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*goh-GHEE-bik*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") or [/ɡoʊˈɡɛbɪk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*goh-GHEH-bik*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") in the [Upper Peninsula](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Upper Peninsula of Michigan") of the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"). As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the population was 14,380.[[2]](#cite_note-QF-2) The [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") is [Bessemer](/wiki/Bessemer,_Michigan "Bessemer, Michigan").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Gogebic County is the [westernmost county](/wiki/List_of_extreme_points_of_U.S._states_and_territories "List of extreme points of U.S. states and territories") in Michigan, and is one of four Michigan counties within the [Central Time Zone](/wiki/Central_Time_Zone "Central Time Zone"). Gogebic County borders [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") to the south, and has a shoreline on [Lake Superior](/wiki/Lake_Superior "Lake Superior") to the north.

**Gogebic County** ([/ɡoʊˈɡiːbɪk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*goh-GHEE-bik*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") or [/ɡoʊˈɡɛbɪk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*goh-GHEH-bik*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is a [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)") in the [Upper Peninsula](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Upper Peninsula of Michigan") of the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"). As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the population was 14,380.[[2]](#cite_note-QF-2) The [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") is [Bessemer](/wiki/Bessemer,_Michigan "Bessemer, Michigan").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Gogebic County is the [westernmost county](/wiki/List_of_extreme_points_of_U.S._states_and_territories "List of extreme points of U.S. states and territories") in Michigan, and is one of four Michigan counties within the [Central Time Zone](/wiki/Central_Time_Zone "Central Time Zone"). Gogebic County borders [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") to the south, and has a shoreline on [Lake Superior](/wiki/Lake_Superior "Lake Superior") to the north.

Gogebic County has long been territory of the [Lake Superior Chippewa](/wiki/Lake_Superior_Chippewa "Lake Superior Chippewa"). The [Lac Vieux Desert Indian Reservation](/wiki/Lac_Vieux_Desert_Indian_Reservation "Lac Vieux Desert Indian Reservation") is located within Gogebic County.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gogebic_County,_Michigan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Gogebic County was organized in 1887, partitioned from [Ontonagon County](/wiki/Ontonagon_County,_Michigan "Ontonagon County, Michigan"). The county's name derives from a lake of the same name, which was originally rendered *Agogebic*. Sources agree that the name is from [Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe_language "Ojibwe language"), but differ on the original meaning. The county's website suggests it meant "body of water hanging on high,"[[4]](#cite_note-4) but an 1884 military annal said it meant "water-mold lake" (*Agogibing*).[[5]](#cite_note-5) (See also: [List of place names of Native American origin in Michigan](/wiki/List_of_place_names_of_Native_American_origin_in_Michigan "List of place names of Native American origin in Michigan"))

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gogebic_County,_Michigan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Lake_Gogebic.JPG/250px-Lake_Gogebic.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lake_Gogebic.JPG)

Lake Gogebic, the largest lake of the Upper Peninsula of Michigan, lies partially in Gogebic County.

According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the county has a total area of 1,476 square miles (3,820 km2), of which 1,102 square miles (2,850 km2) is land and 374 square miles (970 km2) (25%) is water.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Water features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gogebic_County,_Michigan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Water features")]

* [Lake Superior](/wiki/Lake_Superior "Lake Superior") – forms the county's northern land border.
* [Lake Gogebic](/wiki/Lake_Gogebic "Lake Gogebic") – the largest lake in the [Upper Peninsula](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula "Upper Peninsula").
* [Montreal River](/wiki/Montreal_River_(Wisconsin-Michigan) "Montreal River (Wisconsin-Michigan)") – forms the county's western border, as well as the state's border with [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") in this area.

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gogebic_County,_Michigan&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the county had a population of 14,380.[[12]](#cite_note-Census2020PL-12) The median age was 51.0 years, 16.5% of residents were under the age of 18, and 28.0% of residents were 65 years of age or older. For every 100 females there were 103.1 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 103.5 males age 18 and over.[[13]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-13)

The racial makeup of the county was 90.8% [White](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans"), 0.5% [Black or African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 3.6% [American Indian and Alaska Native](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), 0.2% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), <0.1% [Native Hawaiian](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians") and [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander "Pacific Islander"), 0.5% from some other race, and 4.3% from [two or more races](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans"). [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") residents of any race comprised 1.5% of the population.[[12]](#cite_note-Census2020PL-12)

35.9% of residents lived in urban areas, while 64.1% lived in rural areas.[[14]](#cite_note-Census2020DHC-14)

There were 6,874 households in the county, of which 19.1% had children under the age of 18 living in them. Of all households, 41.3% were married-couple households, 25.2% were households with a male householder and no spouse or partner present, and 25.0% were households with a female householder and no spouse or partner present. About 38.0% of all households were made up of individuals and 17.7% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[13]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-13)

There were 10,393 housing units, of which 33.9% were vacant. Among occupied housing units, 77.8% were owner-occupied and 22.2% were renter-occupied. The homeowner vacancy rate was 2.9% and the rental vacancy rate was 17.1%.[[13]](#cite_note-Census2020DP-13)

### American Community Survey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gogebic_County,_Michigan&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: American Community Survey")]

Its population remained predominantly non-Hispanic white, and the largest ancestries as of 2022's [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") were German (16.5%), Italian (13.6%), English (9.5%), and Polish (7.9%).[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gogebic_County,_Michigan&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

The [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") indicated Gogebic County had a population of 16,427.[[16]](#cite_note-16) This decrease of 943 people from the 2000 United States census was a -5.4% change in population. In 2010, there were 7,037 households and 4,171 families residing in the county. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 15 people per square mile (5.8 people/km2). There were 10,795 housing units at an average density of 10 per square mile (3.9/km2).

Among them, 91.7% of the population were [White](/wiki/White_American "White American"), 4.1% [Black or African American](/wiki/African_American "African American"), 2.4% [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), 0.2% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_American "Asian American"), 0.2% of some other race and 1.4% [of two or more races](/wiki/Multiracial_American "Multiracial American"). 0.9% were [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") (of any race). 17.4% were of [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_American "Finnish American"), 12.8% [German](/wiki/German_American "German American"), 11.2% [American](/wiki/American_ethnicity "American ethnicity"), 10.0% [Italian](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American"), 6.8% [Polish](/wiki/Polish_American "Polish American"), 6.7% [English](/wiki/English_American "English American") and 5.1% [Irish](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American") ancestry.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In 2010, there were 7,037 households, out of which 20.6% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 45.4% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 9.3% had a female householder with no husband present, and 40.7% were non-families. 35.3% of all households were made up of individuals, and 16.5% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.11 and the average family size was 2.69. Its county population was 16.9% under the age of 18, 8.4% from 18 to 24, 22.2% from 25 to 44, 31.1% from 45 to 64, and 21.5% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 46.8 years. 53.3% of the population was male, and 46.7% of the population was female.

As of 2010, the median income for a household in the county was $34,917, and the median income for a family was $47,219. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the county was $20,759. About 14.0% of families and 17.2% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 28.6% of those under age 18 and 9.0% of those age 65 or over.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gogebic_County,_Michigan&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Sports")]

Gogebic is the only known place in the Upper Peninsula to ever host a professional sports team, with Ironwood hosting the [Northwest Football League](/wiki/Northwest_Football_League "Northwest Football League")'s Gogebic Panthers in 1935[[18]](#cite_note-18) and 1936. Their 1935 campaign was immensely successful as the Panthers finished with a 6–1 record, their only loss coming to the eventual league champion [La Crosse Old Style Lagers](/w/index.php?title=La_Crosse_Old_Style_Lagers&action=edit&redlink=1 "La Crosse Old Style Lagers (page does not exist)").[[19]](#cite_note-19) Their 1936 campaign featured a 6–0 victory over the Madison Cardinals, who folded a few days after the Lagers defeated them 100–0 in an effort to kick them out of the league.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gogebic_County,_Michigan&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Politics")]

Gogebic County was reliably Republican at the beginning of the twentieth century. Roosevelt's [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") changed the county's mood, which had voted for the Democratic candidate in all but 2 presidential elections between 1932 and 2012. In 2016, [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") became the first Republican to win the county since [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") did in 1972. Trump carried the county again in 2020, despite losing nationally, and again in 2024, by a larger margin each time.

The county is strongly liberal on economic issues and also tends to be somewhat liberal on cultural issues, voting in favor of [Michigan Proposal 2](/wiki/2022_Michigan_Proposal_2 "2022 Michigan Proposal 2") and [Michigan Proposal 3](/wiki/2022_Michigan_Proposal_3 "2022 Michigan Proposal 3"), which loosened voter restrictions and codified abortion rights, respectively. Both referendums passed statewide but the latter failed in most counties in the Upper Peninsula. However, the county's heavily unionized electorate is significantly more conservative on other issues, such as affirmative action, as over 70% of Gogebic County residents voted in favor of the Republican-led [Michigan Civil Rights Initiative](/wiki/Michigan_Civil_Rights_Initiative "Michigan Civil Rights Initiative"), which banned affirmative action.

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gogebic_County,_Michigan&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/BessemerCH1920.jpg/250px-BessemerCH1920.jpg)](/wiki/File:BessemerCH1920.jpg)

Gogebic County Courthouse circa 1920

The county government operates the [jail](/wiki/County_jail "County jail"), maintains rural roads, operates the major local courts, maintains [vital records](/wiki/Vital_records "Vital records") and property records, administers [public health](/wiki/Public_health "Public health") regulations, and participates with the state in the provision of welfare and other social services. The [county board of commissioners](/wiki/County_commission "County commission") controls the budget and has limited authority to make laws or ordinances. In Michigan, most local government functions—police and fire, building and zoning, tax assessment, street maintenance, etc.—are the responsibility of individual cities and townships.
